summary: Gruber offers rapid-fire commentary on Samsung's February 2019 Unpacked event, where the company introduced the Galaxy Fold, the S10 lineup, new wearables, and wireless earbuds. He dismisses the Galaxy Fold as a prototype rather than a real product, and questions the S10 5G's relevance given nonexistent 5G networks. He draws repeated comparisons to Apple's product philosophy, arguing Samsung is clinging to fingerprint sensors and home buttons partly out of technical limitation rather than conviction. He also critiques Samsung's decision to integrate Instagram into the system Camera app as tone-deaf amid growing privacy concerns about Facebook.
key_points: The Galaxy Fold looks like a prototype, not a shipping product — foldable displays have potential but this isn't the realization.
Samsung's continued use of fingerprint sensors over facial recognition is partly philosophical but largely a technical shortcoming — they lack the software chops for a Face ID equivalent.
Phones are becoming the primary computing device for more people, making features like reverse wireless charging and on-device video editing increasingly sensible.
Samsung's decision to bake Instagram into the system Camera app is tone-deaf given growing public concern about Facebook's privacy practices.
Apple's clean break with the iPhone X experience was the right call, but familiarity has strong appeal — Samsung is exploiting that, though risking stagnation.
money_quotes: I think flexible, foldable displays have a good future, but this isn't it.
What's the pitch here? Get yourself a too-big phone for a network that doesn't yet exist?
Familiarity has a strong appeal, but stick with it too long and you risk stagnation.
A big reason why Samsung is sticking with fingerprints rather than facial recognition is that I don't think they have the software chops to do facial recognition well — reliable, quickly, securely.
This is one of the dumbest ideas in the industry — although with the S10 you can finally reassign the button to launch something else, like, say, the Camera app.
Having just one pocket-sized device that serves as both your 4K camera and your editing workstation is just amazing.
The world is growing ever more wary of the privacy invasiveness of Facebook, and Samsung decides 2019 is the right time to bake Facebook's Instagram right into the system Camera app?
Why the hell does Samsung brand all these things 'Galaxy' anyway?
key_insight: Samsung's Unpacked event revealed a company hedging on the future — shipping gimmicky prototypes while clinging to familiar design choices it can't technically move beyond.
